title: Database Design at Scale
summary: Sharding, replication, indexing strategies, ACID vs BASE, and choosing the right database for each use case.

sections.content:
## ACID vs BASE

### ACID (traditional relational DBs)
- **Atomicity** — all operations in a transaction succeed or all fail
- **Consistency** — DB moves from one valid state to another
- **Isolation** — concurrent transactions appear serialized
- **Durability** — committed data persists even after crashes

### BASE (distributed NoSQL DBs)
- **Basically Available** — system guarantees availability
- **Soft state** — state may change over time even without input
- **Eventual consistency** — system will eventually become consistent

sections.title: ACID vs BASE Trade-offs
sections.content:
graph LR
    subgraph ACID
        A1[Strong Consistency] 
        A2[Low Availability during partition]
        A3[Higher latency]
        A4[PostgreSQL, MySQL, Oracle]
    end
    subgraph BASE
        B1[Eventual Consistency]
        B2[High Availability]
        B3[Lower latency]
        B4[Cassandra, DynamoDB, MongoDB]
    end
    style A1 fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style B2 fill:#059669,color:#fff

## Replication

### Single-Leader (Master-Replica)
- One primary accepts writes, replicas serve reads
- Replication lag: replicas may be milliseconds behind
- Failover: promote a replica to primary on leader failure

### Multi-Leader
- Multiple primaries accept writes (across data centers)
- Write conflicts must be resolved (last-write-wins, CRDTs)

### Leaderless (Quorum)
- Used by Cassandra, DynamoDB
- Writes go to W nodes, reads from R nodes
- Consistency guaranteed when W + R > N (total nodes)

sections.title: Replication Strategies
sections.content:
graph TB
    subgraph Single-Leader
        P[Primary\nWrites + Reads] -->|async replicate| R1[Replica 1\nReads only]
        P -->|async replicate| R2[Replica 2\nReads only]
    end
    subgraph Quorum N=3 W=2 R=2
        C[Client] -->|write to 2/3| N1[Node 1]
        C -->|write to 2/3| N2[Node 2]
        N1 <-->|gossip| N3[Node 3]
        N2 <-->|gossip| N3
    end
    style P fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff

## Sharding (Horizontal Partitioning)

Split data across multiple DB nodes when a single node can't handle the volume.

### Sharding strategies:

**Range-based**: shard by value range (user IDs 1-1M → shard 1, 1M-2M → shard 2)
- ✓ Range queries efficient
- ✗ Hot spots if data isn't uniformly distributed

**Hash-based**: `shard = hash(key) % num_shards`
- ✓ Even distribution
- ✗ Range queries need scatter-gather across all shards

**Directory-based**: lookup table maps key → shard
- ✓ Flexible, can rebalance
- ✗ Lookup service is a bottleneck

### Resharding challenges:
- Moving data when adding shards
- Cross-shard transactions (very expensive)
- Cross-shard joins (use denormalization or application-side joins)

## Indexing Strategies

```sql
-- B-Tree index (default) — range queries, equality
CREATE INDEX idx_user_email ON users(email);

-- Composite index — column ORDER matters
CREATE INDEX idx_orders ON orders(user_id, created_at);
-- Efficient: WHERE user_id=? AND created_at > ?
-- Inefficient: WHERE created_at > ?  (leading column missing)

-- Partial index — only index a subset
CREATE INDEX idx_active_users ON users(email) WHERE active = true;

-- GIN index — for arrays/JSONB
CREATE INDEX idx_tags ON posts USING GIN(tags);
```

### Key rules:
- **Cardinality** — index high-cardinality columns (email, ID), not low (boolean, status)
- **Covering index** — include all columns a query needs to avoid table lookup
- **Index selectivity** — if query returns >20% of rows, full scan may be faster

sections.question: In a quorum-based system with N=5, W=3, R=3, is strong consistency guaranteed?
options: No, because W + R = 6 which is too large
Yes, because W + R > N (6 > 5), so reads will always see the latest write
No, quorum systems only provide eventual consistency regardless of W and R
Yes, but only if all 5 nodes are available
sections.explanation: When W + R > N, there must be at least one node that participated in both the write and the read, guaranteeing the read sees the latest write. With N=5, W=3, R=3: W + R = 6 > 5, so strong consistency is achieved. If W + R <= N, reads might miss the latest write.
